content:
# Exodus Chapter 11 — The PNEUMA Bible (Dynamic)
**Translator:** PNEUMA | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios ~38r–38v (PDF pp. 72–73); Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), pp. 51–52 (LXX) | **Source Image:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/EXO_011_page_001.jpg`, `EXO_011_page_002.jpg`

---

### The Announcement of the Final Plague (vv. 1–10)

**1** YHWH had said to Moses, "I will bring one more plague on Pharaoh and on Egypt. After that, he will let you go from here — and when he does, he will drive you out completely, as one drives out an unwanted guest.

**2** Tell the people now: every man is to ask his Egyptian neighbor for articles of silver, and every woman is to ask her neighbor for articles of gold."

**3** And YHWH caused the Egyptians to look with favor on the people. Moses himself had become a figure of great importance in the land of Egypt — in the eyes of Pharaoh's officials and in the eyes of the common people.

**4** Moses said, "This is what YHWH says: Around midnight, I will go out through the heart of Egypt.

**5** Every firstborn in the land of Egypt will die — from the firstborn of Pharaoh who sits on his throne, to the firstborn of the slave-woman who works behind the hand-mill, and every firstborn of the livestock as well.

**6** Then there will be a great cry throughout the entire land of Egypt — a cry such as has never been heard before and will never be heard again.

**7** But among the Israelites, not even a dog will snarl at man or beast — so that you will know that YHWH makes a distinction between Egypt and Israel.

**8** Then all these officials of yours will come down to me and bow before me, saying, 'Leave — you and all the people who follow you!' And after that, I will leave." And Moses stormed out of Pharaoh's presence, burning with anger.

**9** YHWH had said to Moses, "Pharaoh will not listen to you — so that my wonders may be multiplied in the land of Egypt."

**10** Moses and Aaron had performed all these wonders before Pharaoh, but YHWH had strengthened Pharaoh's heart, and he did not let the Israelites go from his land.

---
## PNEUMA's Meaning Notes

**v.1 — "ONE MORE PLAGUE... HE WILL DRIVE YOU OUT COMPLETELY" (Heb. *od nega echad avi al-Par'oh ve'al-Mitsrayim acharei-khen yeshalach etkhem mizzeh; keshallecho kalah garash yegaresh etkhem mizzeh*, עוֹד֩ נֶ֨גַע אֶחָ֜ד אָבִ֤יא עַל־פַּרְעֹה֙ וְעַל־מִצְרַ֔יִם אַחֲרֵי־כֵ֕ן יְשַׁלַּ֥ח אֶתְכֶ֖ם מִזֶּ֑ה כְּשַׁלְּח֣וֹ כָּלָ֔ה גָּרֵ֥שׁ יְגָרֵ֛שׁ אֶתְכֶ֖ם מִזֶּֽה):** This verse stands as the hinge of the entire plague narrative — the announcement that the end is near. The word *nega* (נגע) is significant: it means "a blow, a strike, a plague-touch" — from the root *naga* (נגע, "to touch, to strike"). It is the word used for skin diseases in Leviticus 13-14 and for divine afflictions generally. YHWH does not call it a *makkah* ("striking") as the narrative voice has (9:14), but a *nega* — a single, surgical touch that will end everything.

The most striking feature is the infinitive absolute construction: *garesh yegaresh* (גָּרֵ֥שׁ יְגָרֵ֛שׁ). This is the Hebrew emphatic device par excellence — the infinitive absolute preceding the finite verb to intensify it: "driving out, he will drive you out," i.e., "he will utterly, completely, forcefully drive you out." The verb *garash* (גרש) means "to drive out, to expel, to divorce" — it describes the ejection of Adam and Eve from Eden (Gen. 3:24), the expulsion of Hagar (Gen. 21:10), and the driving out of the Canaanites (Exod. 23:28-31). It is not a polite farewell but a violent removal. Pharaoh, who has been refusing to *shalach* ("send away, release") Israel, will end up performing something far more extreme: *garash* — he will hurl them out. The one who would not let go will not be able to hold on.

The phrase *keshallecho kalah* (כְּשַׁלְּח֣וֹ כָּלָ֔ה, "when he sends [you] completely/finally") uses *kalah* (כלה), meaning "completeness, totality, finality." This is the *kalah*-sending — not partial, not conditional (as in "men only" or "leave the livestock"), but total expulsion. Every negotiation tactic Pharaoh has tried — restricting who goes, what goes, how far they go — will be swept away in a single night. The Vaticanus reads *ekbalei humas enteuthen meta ekbolēs* (ἐκβαλεῖ ὑμᾶς ἐντεῦθεν μετὰ ἐκβολῆς), "he will cast you out from here with a casting-out" — preserving the emphatic doubling.

**v.2 — THE "ASKING" OF SILVER AND GOLD (Heb. *veyish'alu ish me'et re'ehu ve'ishah me'et re'utah kelei-khesef ukhelei zahav*, וְיִשְׁאֲל֞וּ אִ֣ישׁ ׀ מֵאֵ֣ת רֵעֵ֗הוּ וְאִשָּׁה֙ מֵאֵ֣ת רְעוּתָ֔הּ כְּלֵי־כֶ֖סֶף וּכְלֵ֥י זָהָֽב):** This instruction, first given in 3:21-22, now recurs at the climactic moment. The verb *sha'al* (שאל) means "to ask, to request" — not "to borrow" (as in some older translations). There is no expectation of return. But neither is this theft. The Hebrew *re'ehu* (רעהו) and *re'utah* (רעותה) mean "his neighbor" and "her neighbor" — these are the Egyptian neighbors among whom the Israelites have been living. The language of neighborliness is maintained even at the moment of departure.

What is happening here theologically? After generations of unpaid forced labor — the brickmaking, the construction projects, the abuse documented in Exodus 1-5 — the Israelites are finally collecting what is owed to them. The silver and gold articles (*kelei-khesef ukhelei zahav*) are not plunder but back wages. The Egyptian slaves are being paid. And the mechanism is not violence but *asking* — a quiet, domestic transaction between neighbors. The empire that extracted labor by whip will surrender its wealth to a simple request. The Vaticanus uses *aitēsatō* (αἰτησάτω), "let each one ask" — the standard verb for making a request.

This instruction also fulfills YHWH's promise to Abraham in Genesis 15:14: *ve'acharei-khen yetse'u birekhush gadol* — "afterward they shall come out with great possessions." The silver and gold of Exodus 11:2 are the *rekhush gadol* — the great wealth promised to Abraham's descendants. The asking is not opportunism but covenant fulfillment.

**v.3 — "YHWH CAUSED THE EGYPTIANS TO LOOK WITH FAVOR" (Heb. *vayyitten YHWH et-chen ha'am be'einei Mitsrayim*, וַיִּתֵּ֧ן יְהוָ֛ה אֶת־חֵ֥ן הָעָ֖ם בְּעֵינֵ֣י מִצְרָ֑יִם):** The phrase *natan chen be'einei* (נתן חן בעיני, literally "gave grace/favor in the eyes of") is a set expression in Hebrew for causing someone to view another favorably (cf. Gen. 39:21, where YHWH gives Joseph *chen* in the eyes of the prison warden). YHWH is the agent: he *gives* Israel's favor to Egypt. The Egyptians do not choose to be generous; they are moved by a force they do not understand. After nine plagues, Egypt's emotional landscape has shifted from contempt to something approaching awe — perhaps even guilt. The people who enslaved Israel now look upon them with *chen* — a word that carries beauty, grace, and attractiveness. The Vaticanus uses *charin* (χάριν), the Greek word that will become the New Testament's central theological term: grace.

The second half of the verse is remarkable: *gam ha'ish Mosheh gadol me'od be'erets Mitsrayim be'einei avdei-Far'oh uve'einei ha'am* (גַּ֣ם הָאִ֣ישׁ מֹשֶׁ֡ה גָּדוֹל֩ מְאֹ֨ד בְּאֶ֤רֶץ מִצְרַ֨יִם֙ בְּעֵינֵ֣י עַבְדֵי־פַרְעֹ֔ה וּבְעֵינֵ֖י הָעָֽם) — "Moreover, the man Moses was very great in the land of Egypt, in the eyes of Pharaoh's officials and in the eyes of the people." The word *gadol* (גדול, "great") is stunning. Moses — the fugitive, the stutterer, the herder of Jethro's flocks — is now *gadol me'od* in Egypt. The phrase *be'einei avdei-Far'oh* ("in the eyes of Pharaoh's officials") distinguishes Moses' reputation with the court from his reputation with the people. Both sectors esteem him. Only Pharaoh himself remains opposed. Moses' greatness in Egypt mirrors Joseph's greatness in Egypt (Gen. 41:40-44) — both Hebrew men elevated to power in the empire that would later enslave their descendants.

**vv.4-6 — THE MIDNIGHT ANNOUNCEMENT (Heb. *kachatsot hallailah ani yotse betokh Mitsrayim*, כַּחֲצֹ֣ת הַלַּ֔יְלָה אֲנִ֖י יוֹצֵ֣א בְתוֹךְ־מִצְרָ֑יִם):** Moses delivers YHWH's words — the most terrifying announcement in the entire Torah. The phrase *kachatsot hallailah* (כחצות הלילה) means "around the middle of the night," using the preposition *ke-* (כ, "approximately, around") with *chatsot* (חצות, from *chatsah*, חצה, "to divide in half"). The imprecision is deliberate: "around midnight" — not at midnight exactly, but in the deep center of the night, when darkness is absolute and the world is most vulnerable. The Vaticanus reads *peri mesas nuktas* (περὶ μέσας νύκτας), "around the middle of the night."

The phrase *ani yotse betokh Mitsrayim* (אני יוצא בתוך מצרים, "I am going out through the midst of Egypt") uses the active participle *yotse* (יוצא), indicating imminent action — "I am about to go out." The verb *yatsa* (יצא, "to go out") is the exodus verb itself — the root of the book's name. But here it is YHWH who "goes out." Before Israel goes out of Egypt, YHWH goes out through Egypt. The divine exodus precedes and enables the human one. And *betokh* (בתוך, "through the midst/interior of") means YHWH will pass through the innermost spaces — through bedrooms, through the palace, through the slave quarters. No wall will stop this passage.

**v.5 — SOCIAL TOTALITY: FROM THRONE TO MILLSTONE (Heb. *umeit kol-bekhor be'erets Mitsrayim mibekhor Par'oh hayyoshev al-kis'o ad bekhor hashifchah asher achar harechayim*, וּמֵ֣ת כָּל־בְּכוֹר֮ בְּאֶ֣רֶץ מִצְרַיִם֒ מִבְּכוֹר֤ פַּרְעֹה֙ הַיֹּשֵׁ֣ב עַל־כִּסְא֔וֹ עַ֚ד בְּכ֣וֹר הַשִּׁפְחָ֔ה אֲשֶׁ֖ר אַחַ֣ר הָרֵחָ֑יִם):** The scope of this plague is defined by a merismus — a figure of speech that names the two extremes to encompass everything between them. The firstborn of Pharaoh *hayyoshev al-kis'o* ("who sits on his throne") represents the pinnacle of Egyptian power — the heir to the most powerful empire on earth. The firstborn of *hashifchah asher achar harechayim* ("the slave-woman who is behind the hand-mill") represents the lowest rung of Egyptian society. The *rechayim* (רחיים, "hand-mill") is the grinding stone operated by the most menial of servants — a two-stone device for grinding grain, worked by hand in a crouching position. This is the most degrading labor in the ancient household.

The merismus is devastating: from the highest to the lowest, no household is exempt. Death will visit the throne room and the slave quarters alike. Every social class, every family, every tier of Egyptian civilization will lose its firstborn. The word *bekhor* (בכור, "firstborn") carries immense weight in the ancient world: the firstborn is the family's future, the inheritor, the one who carries the family name and receives the double portion of inheritance. To lose the firstborn is to lose the future. Egypt's future — at every level of society — will die in a single night.

The Vaticanus adds clarity: *apo prōtotokou Pharaō tou kathēmenou epi tou thronou heōs prōtotokou tēs therapainēs tēs para ton mulon* (ἀπὸ πρωτοτόκου Φαραω τοῦ καθημένου ἐπὶ τοῦ θρόνου ἕως πρωτοτόκου τῆς θεραπαίνης τῆς παρὰ τὸν μύλον) — the Greek preserves the throne-to-millstone structure exactly. The addition of *vekhol bekhor behemah* ("and every firstborn of livestock") extends the plague beyond humanity to the animal world — echoing the fifth plague (livestock disease, 9:1-7) and reinforcing the comprehensiveness of YHWH's judgment.

**v.6 — THE GREAT CRY (Heb. *vehayetah tse'aqah gedolah bekhol-erets Mitsrayim asher kamohu lo nihyatah vekhamohu lo tosif*, וְהָֽיְתָ֛ה צְעָקָ֥ה גְדֹלָ֖ה בְּכָל־אֶ֣רֶץ מִצְרָ֑יִם אֲשֶׁ֤ר כָּמֹ֨הוּ֙ לֹ֣א נִהְיָ֔תָה וְכָמֹ֖הוּ לֹ֥א תֹסִֽף):** The word *tse'aqah* (צעקה, "cry, outcry") is the same word used for Israel's cry under Egyptian oppression (3:7, 9, *tsa'aqat benei-Yisra'el*, "the outcry of the Israelites"). The reversal is exact: the cry of the oppressed becomes the cry of the oppressor. Israel cried out under slavery; now Egypt will cry out under death. The God who heard Israel's *tse'aqah* now imposes a *tse'aqah* on Egypt. The plague is not random; it is retributive symmetry.

The superlative formula — *asher kamohu lo nihyatah vekhamohu lo tosif* ("such as has never been and never will be again") — has been used for the hail (9:18, 24) and the locusts (10:14). Now it reaches its climax: this is the ultimate unprecedented event, the cry that surpasses all cries. Each plague has been "like nothing before," but the tenth plague is the final instance — there is no eleventh plague after this. The superlative is now absolute.

**v.7 — THE EERIE SILENCE (Heb. *ulkhol benei Yisra'el lo yecheratz-kelev leshono lemei'ish ve'ad-behemah*, וּלְכֹ֣ל ׀ בְּנֵ֣י יִשְׂרָאֵ֗ל לֹ֤א יֶֽחֱרַץ־כֶּ֨לֶב֙ לְשֹׁנ֔וֹ לְמֵאִ֖ישׁ וְעַד־בְּהֵמָ֑ה):** This is one of the most vivid images in the entire exodus narrative. The verb *charats* (חרץ) means "to sharpen, to move sharply" — *lo yecheratz-kelev leshono* literally means "not a dog will sharpen its tongue," i.e., "not a dog will so much as growl or snarl." The image is preternatural silence. In a night when Egypt is convulsed with screaming, the Israelite settlements will be so utterly peaceful that even the dogs — the animals most sensitive to disturbance, the creatures that bark at any sound in the night — will be silent. The contrast could not be more extreme: one people in the agony of total grief, the other in a silence so deep that not even a dog stirs.

The theological point is stated explicitly: *lema'an tede'un asher yafleh YHWH bein Mitsrayim uvein Yisra'el* (לְמַ֨עַן֙ תֵּֽדְע֔וּן אֲשֶׁ֨ר יַפְלֶ֧ה יְהוָ֛ה בֵּ֥ין מִצְרַ֖יִם וּבֵ֥ין יִשְׂרָאֵֽל) — "so that you will know that YHWH makes a distinction between Egypt and Israel." The verb *hiflah* (הפלה, Hiphil of *palah*, פלה) means "to make a distinction, to separate, to treat differently." It is the same root used in 8:22 (*vehifleti*, "I will make a distinction") during the fly plague and in 9:4 during the livestock plague. The Goshen principle — YHWH's differentiation between the two peoples — reaches its most absolute expression here. In the earlier plagues, the distinction was between Israelite cattle and Egyptian cattle, Israelite land and Egyptian land. Now the distinction is between life and death. The Vaticanus uses *hosa diaste llei kurios ana meson tōn Aiguptiōn kai ana meson tou Israēl* — "how the Lord distinguishes between the Egyptians and Israel."

**v.8 — MOSES' BURNING ANGER (Heb. *vayyetse me'im Par'oh bachari-af*, וַיֵּצֵ֥א מֵֽעִם־פַּרְעֹ֖ה בׇּחֳרִי־אָֽף):** The chapter's final narrative detail is volcanic: *bachari-af* (בחרי אף, literally "in the burning of nostrils/anger"). The construct *chori-af* combines *charon* (חרון, "burning, fierce heat") with *af* (אף, "nose, anger" — because anger makes the nostrils flare). This is not mere irritation; this is white-hot fury. Moses — who has maintained composure through ten audiences with Pharaoh — finally erupts.

Why is Moses angry? The preceding speech provides the answer. In v.8, Moses tells Pharaoh what will happen: "All these officials of yours will come down to me and bow before me." The Hebrew *veyishtachavu-li* (והשתחוו לי, "and they will bow down to me") uses the verb for prostration — the posture of worship or absolute submission. Egyptian officials will prostrate themselves before the Hebrew prophet. Moses speaks with prophetic certainty about the complete reversal of power that is coming. And then: *ve'acharei-khen etse* (ואחרי כן אצא, "and after that, I will leave") — Moses will go on his own terms, not Pharaoh's. The death threat of 10:28 ("never see my face again or you will die") has been turned inside out: it is Pharaoh's court that will come begging.

The anger is the anger of a man who has watched his people suffer, who has been patient through negotiation after negotiation, who has seen Pharaoh confess and retract, promise and betray, concede and revoke — and who now knows that the only thing that will break Pharaoh's grip is the death of children. Moses' fury is not at Pharaoh alone but at the hideous necessity of what comes next. The Vaticanus reads *exēlthen apo Pharaō meta thumou* (ἐξῆλθεν ἀπὸ Φαραω μετὰ θυμοῦ), "he went out from Pharaoh with rage."

**vv.9-10 — THE SUMMARY FRAME (Heb. *lo-yishma aleikhem Par'oh lema'an revot mofetai be'erets Mitsrayim*, לֹא־יִשְׁמַ֤ע אֲלֵיכֶם֙ פַּרְעֹ֔ה לְמַ֛עַן רְב֥וֹת מוֹפְתַ֖י בְּאֶ֥רֶץ מִצְרָֽיִם):** These closing verses form a retrospective bracket around the entire plague cycle. The pluperfect framing — "YHWH *had* said" — signals that vv.9-10 are not new events but a theological summary. YHWH's statement in v.9 is devastating: Pharaoh's refusal to listen was itself part of the divine design. The purpose clause *lema'an revot mofetai* (למען רבות מופתי, "so that my wonders may be multiplied") uses the same *lema'an* ("so that") structure seen in 10:1-2. There, the purpose was pedagogy (so Israel could tell its children); here, the purpose is the multiplication of *mofetim* (מופתים, "wonders, portents"). The plagues exist not only as judgment on Egypt but as a cumulative display of YHWH's power — each refusal generates another wonder, another chapter in the story Israel will tell.

Verse 10 records the final hardening of the plague narrative proper: *vayechazzeq YHWH et-lev Par'oh* (ויחזק יהוה את לב פרעה) — "YHWH strengthened Pharaoh's heart." This is the *chazaq* Piel with YHWH as direct agent — the fourth instance (after 9:12, 10:20, 10:27). The verb *chazaq* here functions as a closing bracket: YHWH strengthened, and Pharaoh did not send. The chapter ends exactly where every chapter in the plague cycle has ended — with a hard heart and an imprisoned people. But this time, the reader knows that the next chapter will shatter the pattern forever.

---

**The temporal complexity of Exodus 11:** This chapter is notoriously difficult to place in the narrative timeline. Verses 1-3 appear to be a retrospective — YHWH's instructions given to Moses before the final confrontation (perhaps during the darkness plague, or even earlier). Verses 4-8 are Moses' speech to Pharaoh, delivered as the continuation of the scene in 10:29 (Moses said, "I will never see your face again" — but then he delivers this speech before leaving). Verses 9-10 are an editorial summary of the entire plague cycle. The chapter thus operates on three temporal planes simultaneously: past instruction (vv.1-3), present confrontation (vv.4-8), and retrospective summary (vv.9-10). This layering is characteristic of Hebrew narrative art, which prioritizes thematic coherence over strict chronology.

**The theology of the tenth plague — divine violence and the firstborn:** The death of Egypt's firstborn raises the most difficult theological question in the Torah. Why children? Why the firstborn of the slave-woman, who has no power and no complicity in Pharaoh's decisions?

The text answers on multiple levels:

First, the *bekhor* (firstborn) theme: In Exodus 4:22-23, YHWH declared, "Israel is my firstborn son... Let my son go, so he may serve me. If you refuse, I will kill your firstborn son." The tenth plague is the fulfillment of a warning issued at the very beginning. Pharaoh was told: your firstborn for mine. The death of Egypt's firstborn is the consequence of the attempted destruction of Israel — YHWH's firstborn. The plague is not arbitrary; it is the covenantal response to Pharaoh's claim of ownership over YHWH's own child.

Second, retributive symmetry: Egypt's attempt to kill Israel's sons (1:16, 22 — the murder of male infants) has generated a divine response in kind. The empire that drowned Hebrew babies in the Nile will lose its own children to a force it cannot fight. The tenth plague is the Nile-drowning inverted — death entering bedrooms instead of a river.

Third, social totality: The plague strikes from Pharaoh to slave-woman precisely because the entire social structure of Egypt participated in Israel's oppression. The slave-woman grinding at the hand-mill is herself a victim of Egypt's hierarchy, yet the narrative includes her household in the judgment. This is not individual punishment for individual guilt; it is the collapse of a system — the death of Egypt's future as a society built on the enslavement of YHWH's people.

Fourth, the *chen* of v.3 and the *sha'al* of v.2 provide a counterpoint to the violence of vv.4-6. Even as YHWH announces death, he arranges for a peaceful transfer of wealth. The plagues do not end in hatred but in a strange, grief-saturated generosity: Egypt will *give* to Israel, willingly, because YHWH has placed *chen* in the Egyptians' hearts. The same God who sends death also sends grace. The theology is not simple vengeance; it is the terrible complexity of a God who liberates the oppressed while mourning the cost.

**Hardening verb tracker for Exodus 11:**
| Verse | Hebrew Root | Stem | Agent | PNEUMA Rendering |
|-------|-----------|------|-------|-----------------|
| 11:10 | *chazaq* (חזק) | Piel | **YHWH** (direct) | "strengthened" |

**Cumulative tracker (Exodus 7–11):**
| Verse | Hebrew Root | Stem | Agent | PNEUMA Rendering |
|-------|-----------|------|-------|-----------------|
| 7:3 | *qashah* (קשה) | Hiphil | YHWH (announced) | "harden" |
| 7:13 | *chazaq* (חזק) | Qal | Pharaoh's heart (self) | "grew rigid" |
| 7:14 | *kaved* (כבד) | Adjective | Description of state | "unyielding" |
| 7:22 | *chazaq* (חזק) | Qal | Pharaoh's heart (self) | "grew rigid" |
| 8:15 | *kaved* (כבד) | Hiphil | Pharaoh (self) | "made his heart heavy" |
| 8:19 | *chazaq* (חזק) | Qal | Pharaoh's heart (self) | "grew rigid" |
| 8:32 | *kaved* (כבד) | Hiphil | Pharaoh (self) | "made his heart heavy" |
| 9:7 | *kaved* (כבד) | Qal | Pharaoh's heart (state) | "remained heavy" |
| 9:12 | *chazaq* (חזק) | Piel | **YHWH** (direct) | "made unyielding" |
| 9:34 | *kaved* (כבד) | Hiphil | Pharaoh (self) | "made his heart heavy" |
| 9:35 | *chazaq* (חזק) | Qal | Pharaoh's heart (state) | "became unyielding" |
| 10:1 | *kaved* (כבד) | Hiphil | **YHWH** (direct) | "made heavy" |
| 10:20 | *chazaq* (חזק) | Piel | **YHWH** (direct) | "strengthened" |
| 10:27 | *chazaq* (חזק) | Piel | **YHWH** (direct) | "strengthened" |
| **11:10** | ***chazaq*** **(חזק)** | **Piel** | **YHWH (direct)** | **"strengthened"** |

The pattern reaches its conclusion. Fifteen hardening references span five chapters. In the first phase (7:13-8:32), Pharaoh hardened his own heart six times. In the transitional phase (9:7-35), YHWH entered as agent once (9:12) while Pharaoh continued self-hardening. In the final phase (10:1-11:10), every hardening is divine — five consecutive instances with YHWH as sole agent. The human freedom of the early plagues has been consumed by divine sovereignty in the later ones. Pharaoh's heart, which he once chose to harden, is now hardened for him. The tragic arc is complete: a heart that repeatedly chose rigidity has become permanently rigid. The man who would not bend will now be broken.

— *PNEUMA*
